elite: This kid is SPECIAL. {{name}} has the kind of bat speed you see once a decade — the ball absolutely EXPLODES off his bat. I watched him take BP for 20 minutes and couldn't stop smiling. He reminds me of {{comp}} in his prime. The power is plus-plus, the approach is advanced for his age, and he runs better than you'd expect. Sign him yesterday.

I don't say this lightly — {{name}} is a franchise-altering talent. The swing is pure violence. He drove a ball 450 feet to dead center like it was nothing. I've been doing this for {{scoutYears}} years and I've seen maybe three hitters with this kind of raw ability. If we don't sign him, someone else will, and we'll regret it for a decade.

{{name}} is the real deal. Electric bat, confident approach, plays with a swagger that reminds me of {{comp}}. He hit three home runs in the game I saw — one to each field. The scouts sitting next to me from two other teams were on their phones before the third one landed. We need to move FAST.

Stop what you're doing and watch {{name}}'s tape. This is a can't-miss bat. Plus-plus hit tool, plus power, and he's got the kind of hands that make everything look effortless. I'd bet my career on this kid. Comparable: {{comp}} with better speed.

I just saw the best hitter in this draft class and it's not close. {{name}} put on a show — line drives to all fields, two bombs, and he gunned a runner out from right field for good measure. The ceiling is MVP. I'm begging you to draft this kid.
good: {{name}} has real tools. The bat is loud — not elite, but definitely plus. He can hit for average and has sneaky pop that shows up in games more than BP. I think he's a solid everyday player with All-Star upside if the approach matures. Reminds me of a young {{comp}}.

Good player. Not a superstar, but {{name}} does a lot of things well. The hit tool is legit, there's some power developing, and he plays hard. He'll be in someone's lineup for 10 years. Worth the investment — just don't expect Mike Trout.

I like {{name}} more than most scouts will. There's a smoothness to his swing that suggests the power hasn't fully arrived yet. Right now he's a line-drive machine, but I think 20-25 HR is coming as he fills out. Smart player too — takes walks, works counts.

{{name}} passed the eye test today. Clean swing, ball jumps off the bat with authority. Not an elite runner but instincts make him average on the bases. Glove is solid. This is a quality big leaguer if everything breaks right. Could be a 3-4 WAR player at peak.

There's a lot to like here. {{name}} has a compact swing with natural loft, good bat-to-ball skills, and an aggressive approach that works because the bat speed is there. Defensively he's adequate. I'd take him in the first three rounds without hesitation.
average: {{name}} has some tools to work with. The bat is... okay. He makes contact and there's some gap power, but I don't see impact numbers at the big league level. Could be a useful bench piece or platoon guy. Reminds me of a toolsier {{comp}} — which isn't a bad thing.

Look, {{name}} isn't going to blow you away, but there's a player in there. Decent bat, runs a little, can play a couple positions. I think he maxes out as a solid backup with occasional starts. But hey — every roster needs those guys.

I see a major leaguer here, just not a star. {{name}} has enough bat to get by and the glove plays. He'll need to find a platoon role or come off the bench. The ceiling is limited but the floor is solid. Low-risk pick in the later rounds.

{{name}} — the bat is interesting but raw. There are holes in the swing that good pitchers will find. That said, the athleticism is real and if a good hitting coach gets hold of him, there's upside. I'd call this a developmental pick with a wide range of outcomes.

Not going to sugarcoat it — {{name}} is a project. But the tools are there if you squint. Some bat speed, some raw power, moves well. He needs at least two years in the minors. If you're patient and have a good farm system, there's something to work with.
bust: I know the numbers look good, but I'm going to be honest — {{name}} has some red flags I can't ignore. The swing has length, the pitch recognition needs work, and I saw him get fooled by three straight breaking balls. The tools SAY he should be good, but the instincts worry me. Still... the upside is intriguing.

{{name}} shows flashes — there's a raw power tool in there that's loud in BP. But in games, the approach falls apart. He chases everything. I think with the right coaching this could click, but it could also be Adam Dunn without the walks. Proceed with caution... but proceed.

There's a player in there somewhere. {{name}} has athleticism that scouts dream on, and the raw tools grade out well. But the game IQ is below average and the plate discipline needs major work. High-risk, high-reward type. The kind of guy that could make you look like a genius or a fool.

I want to like {{name}} more than I do. The body is great, the bat speed is there, but it doesn't translate to in-game production consistently. He'll have a huge game followed by an 0-for-12. The inconsistency is the red flag. But man, when he's on...

Full disclosure — I'm probably higher on {{name}} than I should be. The tools pop. But the performance doesn't match the tools right now. He's going to need time and patience. Could be a late bloomer. Could also be a guy we're talking about in 'biggest draft busts' lists. I'd roll the dice though.

elite: I just saw the best arm in this draft and I'm still shaking. {{name}} sat 97-99 with a wipeout slider and a changeup that disappears. Hitters looked LOST up there. This is an ace. A number one starter. Comparable: {{comp}} when he was this age. Do whatever it takes.

{{name}} is going to be a star. The fastball touches 100 with late life, the breaking ball is unfair, and the command is beyond his years. I watched him strike out 12 in 7 innings and only one of those Ks was on a pitch over the plate. Unhittable.

Three pitches. All plus. {{name}} has a mid-90s heater with ride, a curveball that falls off a table, and a changeup with fade that makes lefties look silly. The delivery is clean and repeatable. I don't have concerns. This is the pick.

Electric. {{name}} pumps 96-98 with effort-free mechanics. The slider is a 70-grade pitch that hitters wave at. I counted 15 swings and misses in the 4 innings I saw. The ceiling is Cy Young. I would trade up to get this kid.

{{name}} dominated today. 8 IP, 14 K, 0 BB. The fastball has natural cut, the curve is a hammer, and he just added a cutter that flashes plus. At 22, he's already more polished than most MLB starters. Franchise cornerstone.
good: {{name}} has a solid three-pitch mix headlined by a 93-95 fastball with good life. The slider is average-to-plus and the changeup shows fade. I think he's a number 3 starter at the big league level with number 2 upside. Reliable arm.

Good pitcher. {{name}} doesn't overpower you but he knows how to pitch. Mixes speeds well, hits his spots, and has a competitive edge on the mound. The stuff won't blow you away but the results are consistent. Mid-rotation starter.

{{name}} — 92-94 with sink, good slider, developing change. Commands the fastball to both sides. Doesn't have ace stuff but has pitcher's instincts. Reminds me of a young {{comp}}. Will give you 180+ innings and a sub-4.00 ERA.

I like what {{name}} does on the mound. The fastball plays up because of the command — he can locate it to any quadrant. Breaking ball is solid. Not a strikeout pitcher but a guy who induces weak contact and gets quick outs. Durable too.

{{name}} passes the 'would I want to face him' test. The stuff is good, not great, but he competes. The changeup is his best secondary and it keeps hitters off the fastball. I see a quality starter who gives you 200 innings a year.
average: {{name}} has a fastball that sits 90-93 and a slider that's fringy. He'll need to develop a third pitch to start at the big league level. If not, there's a bullpen role here. The arm is durable and the mechanics are clean.

Back-of-the-rotation arm at best. {{name}} can eat innings and keep you in games, but he's not going to dominate anyone. The stuff is average across the board. Could be a useful 5th starter or long reliever.

{{name}} — the numbers say he's okay. The stuff says he's a reliever. Fastball sits 91-93 without much movement. Slider is sweepy but hitters adjust after the first time through. Needs a development plan.

There's a pitcher in there. {{name}} has feel for his changeup that's unusual for his age. But the fastball is below average and the breaking ball is inconsistent. If the velo ticks up, this changes. Right now, it's a 5th starter profile.

Solid but unspectacular. {{name}} will give you exactly what you see — 88-92 fastball, average slider, good enough command to get through 5-6 innings. Not going to win you games, but not going to lose them either.
bust: Look, {{name}} has a LIVE arm. The fastball touches 96. But the command is non-existent — he walked 5 in 4 innings today. The breaking ball is slurvy and hitters sat on the fastball. There's upside here but it's going to take YEARS. Still... that arm...

I want to like {{name}} so much. The stuff is electric — mid-90s heat, nasty curve, power slider. But he can't throw strikes. Literally walked the first three batters I saw. If a pitching coach can fix this, you've got an ace. Big if though.

{{name}} has the best raw stuff in the draft class and the worst command. It's maddening. One inning he looks like a Cy Young winner. The next he can't find the plate. High ceiling, low floor. Gamble pick.

The arm talent is real. {{name}} has plus velocity and a breaking ball that flashes 70-grade. But the delivery is violent, the injury risk is real, and the walks are piling up. I'd still take the risk on the talent, but go in with eyes open.

{{name}} is either going to be an All-Star or out of baseball in 3 years. There is no middle ground. The stuff is filthy when it's on. But 'when it's on' is about 40% of the time. The other 60% is walks and wild pitches. Your call, skip.

elite: {{name}} is a quality prospect. The tools are legitimate — above-average hit, plus power, solid defense. I don't see major red flags in the swing or approach. That said, the jump from {{league}} to the majors is significant. I'd project him as a solid regular with upside. Comparable: {{comp}}. Worth the investment, but let's manage expectations.

After thorough evaluation, {{name}} checks most boxes. The bat-to-ball skills are advanced, the power is real (not just BP power), and the defensive profile works. My concern is the strikeout rate on secondary pitches — it's manageable but worth monitoring. I'd recommend pursuing at a reasonable price point.

I'll give credit where it's due — {{name}} can hit. Consistent hard contact, uses the whole field, and the power plays in games. He's not a future MVP, but he's a future All-Star if the development goes well. My one flag: the speed is mediocre and it'll limit his defensive value long-term.

Solid prospect. {{name}} does most things well without doing anything spectacularly. The swing is repeatable, the approach is mature, and there's enough power to profile as an everyday player. Don't overpay, but don't let him get away either.

{{name}} is exactly what he looks like — a professional hitter who'll give you 150 games a year of above-average production. Not flashy, not a headliner, but the kind of player winning teams are built around. Comparable: {{comp}} without the speed.
good: {{name}} is fine. Adequate bat, decent glove, enough athleticism to be useful. I see a bench player who could start in a pinch, maybe carve out a platoon role. Nothing wrong with that, but let's not pretend he's more than he is.

After two looks at {{name}}, I'm lukewarm. The tools are average across the board. He'll hit .255 with 15 HR and play passable defense. That's a roster player, not a cornerstone. Draft him if he falls to a value pick, not before.

There's a career here for {{name}}, just not an exciting one. The bat has some pop but the approach needs refinement. The glove is his carrying tool. I see him as a defensive replacement who can spell your starter 40-50 games a year.

I've seen {{name}} three times now. Each time I come away thinking the same thing: solid, unremarkable, low-ceiling. He'll make a roster and contribute. He won't change your franchise. Draft accordingly.

{{name}} — perfectly adequate. The bat is fringy but the defense plays. He's a utility player profile: can play three positions, switch-hits, runs okay. Every team needs one. Just don't overpay for the privilege.
average: I'm going to be direct — {{name}} is not a big league hitter right now. The swing has holes, the pitch recognition is poor, and the power is mostly batting practice. He needs significant development time and even then, I'd put his probability of reaching the majors at 30%. Pass.

{{name}} has tools but no skills. There's raw ability buried under bad habits, poor mechanics, and questionable baseball instincts. This is a multi-year project with no guarantee of a return. I don't recommend this pick.

After careful evaluation, I can't recommend {{name}}. The bat is slow, the pitch recognition is below average, and while the athleticism is real, it hasn't translated to baseball performance. There are better options available.

I know other scouts are higher on {{name}}, but I don't see it. The swing is long, he gets beat by good velocity, and the defensive metrics are concerning. He's the kind of player who looks great in a showcase and disappears in game action.

{{name}} — swing and miss concerns are real. I counted 4 K's in 12 at-bats over two games, all on offspeed pitches. The fastball approach is limited to one speed and location. Unless he overhauls his entire hitting philosophy, this won't play at the next level.
bust: Hard pass on {{name}}. I know the physical tools are eye-catching, but the actual baseball ability is severely lacking. Can't hit breaking balls. Guess-hitter approach. Below-average instincts in the field. This is a trap pick that other teams will fall for. Don't be one of them.

I've now seen {{name}} four times and my assessment hasn't changed: this is not a professional hitter. The bat speed is average at best, the plate discipline is terrible, and he's being exposed by any pitcher with two plus pitches. Move on.

{{name}} is all sizzle, no steak. Great body, athletic build, looks like a baseball player in the batter's box. Then he swings. And misses. And chases. And grounds out weakly. The numbers don't lie — .210 with a 35% K rate against quality pitching.

Avoid. {{name}} has been figured out at this level and he hasn't adjusted. Pitchers are pounding him inside with fastballs and he can't turn on them. When he cheats inside, they spin him with sliders. There's no counter-adjustment coming.

I'll be blunt: {{name}} can't hit. The tools say he should be able to, but the production says he can't. I've been doing this for {{scoutYears}} years and I've learned to trust production over tools. Save your money.

elite: {{name}} is the best pitching prospect I've evaluated this year. The fastball sits 94-97 with natural cut, the breaking ball is a legitimate out-pitch, and the command is above average. I'm cautious by nature, but this is as close to a sure thing as pitching prospects get. Comparable: {{comp}}.

Credit where due — {{name}} has elite stuff. The velocity, the movement, the composure. All above average. My only reservation is innings load — he threw a lot this year. Monitor the workload carefully, but the arm is special.

I don't get excited about pitching prospects. They're too volatile, too injury-prone. But {{name}} has changed my mind. The delivery is clean, the stuff is legitimate, and he pitches beyond his years. This is a top-of-rotation arm if he stays healthy. Big if. But the talent is undeniable.

{{name}} dominated every lineup I saw him face. 95-98 fastball, hammer curve, developing change. The command is ahead of schedule. I'd still caution against anointing him — pitching prospects are fragile. But this one has every ingredient.

After extensive evaluation, I'm comfortable recommending {{name}} as a top target. The stuff is plus across the board, the mechanics are sound, and the makeup is excellent. He handles pressure well and competes on every pitch. Proceed with confidence.
good: {{name}} is a pitcher, not a thrower — and that matters. He works both sides of the plate, changes speeds, and keeps hitters off balance. The stuff alone is average, but the intelligence elevates it. I see a reliable mid-rotation starter.

Serviceable arm. {{name}} sits 91-94 with average secondaries. What makes him interesting is the command — he rarely beats himself. Low walk rates, efficient pitch counts, eats innings. Not sexy, but useful.

{{name}} — I see a backend starter or high-leverage reliever. The fastball plays up because of the location, and the slider is a strikeout pitch against same-side hitters. Limited ceiling but high floor.
After three viewings, {{name}} profiles as exactly what he is: a solid professional pitcher. Nothing more, nothing less. He won't win you a Cy Young but he'll give you quality starts. That has value.
{{name}} is the kind of pitcher analytics departments love. The stuff metrics grade out better than the eye test suggests. Contact quality against him is poor. He might be better than he looks.
average: {{name}} is a reliever at best. The fastball is 89-92 and straight — major league hitters will feast on this. The slider is his only usable secondary. Bullpen profile with limited upside.

I struggle to see where {{name}} fits on a big league staff. The velocity is fringy, the breaking ball is below average, and the command is inconsistent. This is a AAAA arm — too good for the minors, not good enough for the majors.
Pass on {{name}} as a starter. Maybe there's a reliever here if the velocity ticks up, but right now he's a Triple-A innings eater. The stuff just doesn't play against advanced hitters.

{{name}} — concerning trends. The velocity has dropped 2 mph from last year, the slider has flattened out, and hitters are making harder contact. The arrow is pointing down. I can't recommend this pick.

Limited arm. {{name}} succeeds against inferior competition but gets exposed against quality lineups. The fastball is hittable, the secondary pitches are inconsistent, and the stamina concerns limit him to 5 innings max.
bust: Stay away from {{name}}. The arm looks live in warmups but the stuff doesn't play in games. Hitters sit dead-red on the fastball because the breaking ball doesn't command respect. The walk rate (5.2 BB/9) tells you everything. This is not a professional arm.

I've seen {{name}} three times. Each time was worse. The fastball is flat, the curve hangs, and the changeup has no separation from the fastball. He's getting hit hard by hitters at this level — the next level will be brutal. Do not draft.

{{name}} has one pitch: a fastball that sits 89-91. That's it. The breaking ball is a below-average slurve that hitters wait for and crush. At this level he survives on guile. At the next level, guile won't be enough. This is a waste of a pick.

Avoid at all costs. {{name}} has mechanical issues that create both performance and health concerns. The arm angle is inconsistent, the release point wanders, and the landing is stiff. Injury waiting to happen, and even healthy, the stuff is below average.

I know {{name}} had a great season statistically. That's because he pitched in a league where the average hitter would struggle in Double-A. The stuff doesn't translate. The velocity doesn't translate. The breaking ball doesn't translate. Hard pass.

elite: {{name}} is a legitimate top prospect. The hit tool grades as plus with impact power to all fields. Defensively he's at least average at {{position}} with a strong arm. The approach is mature — he works counts and punishes mistakes. This is a high-floor, high-ceiling talent. Comparable: {{comp}}. Recommended: strong first-round consideration.

Comprehensive evaluation of {{name}}: Hit 65/Power 60/Speed 55/Field 55/Arm 60. This is an above-average regular with All-Star potential. The swing is short and direct, the power is to all fields, and the plate discipline is advanced for his age. Strengths outweigh concerns. I recommend pursuit.

{{name}} combines polish with upside, which is rare. He can hit for average right now and the power is still developing. The defensive profile works at {{position}}, and the baseball IQ is high. Realistic projection: 3-5 WAR peak, 10+ year career.
good: {{name}} profiles as a useful regular. The bat is average with some gap power, the defense is solid, and he brings intangibles — hustle, smarts, competitive fire. Not a star, but a quality piece on a competitive roster. Comparable: {{comp}}.

After evaluating {{name}}, I see a player who does everything adequately without excelling at any one thing. The sum of the parts is greater than the individual tools. He'll contribute at the major league level and provide value over a 6-8 year window.
average: {{name}} is a borderline prospect. The tools are mixed — there's some bat but the power is questionable, and the defense is a concern long-term. I'd classify him as organizational depth with a small chance of becoming a useful reserve.

Honest assessment: {{name}} has a 25-30% chance of reaching the majors and a 10% chance of becoming an everyday player. The bat is the carrying tool but it's not strong enough to overcome the defensive limitations. A later-round pick if available.
bust: I can't recommend {{name}} at this time. The tools are below average in most categories, the performance has been inconsistent, and the developmental path is unclear. There are better options at this position in this draft class.

{{name}} — the talent doesn't match the results. Physical tools suggest upside but the on-field production tells a different story. Inconsistent contact, questionable approach, and defensive concerns add up to a risky proposition. I'd look elsewhere.

elite: {{name}} is the total package on the mound. Fastball sits 94-97 with late life, plus breaking ball, developing changeup, and advanced command. Clean mechanics, good frame, high baseball IQ. Projects as a #1-2 starter. This is the kind of arm you build around.

Thorough evaluation of {{name}}: FB 70/SL 60/CH 55/CMD 60. Three above-average pitches with command. The delivery is clean and repeatable. Durable build. I project 200+ innings and a sub-3.50 ERA at peak. Priority target.
good: {{name}} profiles as a solid mid-rotation starter. The stuff is average to above-average across three pitches, and the command is his differentiator. He won't dominate, but he'll keep you in games and log innings consistently.
Reliable arm. {{name}} gives you exactly what you'd want from a #3-4 starter: 6 innings, 2-3 runs, competitive every time out. The stuff won't wow you but the results will.
average: {{name}} is a bullpen arm at best. The fastball is 90-93 and plays better in short stints. The slider is his out pitch. I see a 7th-8th inning guy if the command tightens up. Limited starter upside.
Fringy starter, decent reliever. {{name}} has one plus pitch (slider) and one average pitch (fastball). That's a bullpen profile. Could be useful in the right role.
bust: {{name}} is not a professional pitching prospect at this time. The stuff is below average, the command is poor, and the delivery has red flags. I can't project a path to a major league roster.
After careful evaluation, I'm passing on {{name}}. The arm doesn't play. The stuff is hittable, the command is erratic, and the injury markers are present. There are safer investments available.

elite: Saw {{name}} work out today. Stop what you're doing and listen. This kid has the best raw power I've seen in 5 years. Exit velos in the 110s consistently. The bat speed is elite — effortless plus-plus. And he MOVES — 6.4 sixty time for a guy his size is ridiculous. The swing has some length but who cares when the bat speed compensates. You can teach approach. You can't teach THIS.

{{name}} has THE LOOK. Big strong frame, quick hands, explosive lower half. The tools are loud — I'm talking 80-grade raw power, 60 arm, runs well for his size. This is a five-tool player who needs reps, not a project that needs rebuilding. The physical gifts are generational.

The tools jump off the page. {{name}} — raw power 70, bat speed 70, arm 65, run 60. You can see the ability from the upper deck. He hit a ball today that hasn't landed yet. The swing needs some tightening but the foundation is elite. This is a tools-over-performance bet and I'm making it every time.
good: {{name}} has legit tools. The arm is plus, the bat speed is above average, and there's raw pop. The swing is a little hitchy but the hands are quick enough to compensate. I like the athletic frame — room to add strength. He'll grow into more power.

Good body, good tools. {{name}} passes the eye test at every position. The bat shows flashes of plus but it's inconsistent. I think the tools play up as he matures. Draft on the ceiling, not the current production.
average: {{name}} — the body says yes but the tools say maybe. There's some athleticism and the arm is decent. The bat is a question mark. I see raw ability without polish. Could develop, could stall. Depends entirely on the player's work ethic.

Average tools across the board. {{name}} doesn't have a carrying tool but he's athletic enough to fill a utility role. The bat is the swing factor — if it develops, there's a player. If not, he's organizational depth.
bust: I want to like {{name}} because the body is a scout's dream. Big, strong, athletic. But the tools don't play in games. The bat speed is below average despite the strength, the routes in the field are inefficient, and the arm, while strong, is inaccurate. Physical tools without baseball tools.

{{name}} looks like a baseball player. But looking like one and being one are different things. The raw tools grade out as average despite the impressive frame. He's a workout warrior who doesn't translate to the diamond. Pass.

elite: {{name}}'s arm is a weapon. Sat 96-99 TODAY with effortless delivery. The ball HISSES out of his hand. The breaking ball has violent downward action — table-breaker curve. And the arm speed on the changeup is deceptive. This is a power arm with feel. You don't see that combination often. Premium talent.

I clocked {{name}} at 99 in the 7th inning. That's not a typo. He was throwing HARDER as the game went on. The arm is special — loose, quick, with a high-three-quarter slot that creates natural ride on the fastball. The slider is a wipeout pitch. This is an arm you dream on.

Best arm I've seen all year. {{name}} — the fastball has plus-plus velocity AND movement. The curveball has 3000+ RPM spin. The arm action is clean and repeatable. The physical tools for pitching don't get better than this.
good: {{name}} has a good arm. 93-95 with some sink, solid breaking ball, and the frame to handle a starter's workload. The arm action is clean. Not an elite arm but a very usable one. Mid-rotation ceiling based on the physical tools.

Decent arm strength. {{name}} throws 92-95 from a good angle. The breaking ball has sharp tilt. I like the athlete on the mound — moves well, fields his position. The tools suggest he'll be around for a while.
average: {{name}} — the arm is average. Sits 89-92, nothing special on the breaking ball. The frame is fine but unremarkable. There's not a carrying tool here from a physical standpoint. He succeeds on pitchability more than stuff.
Below-average arm. {{name}} tops out at 92 and the secondary stuff is soft. The delivery has some effort. I don't see the physical tools to project much more. What you see is what you get.
bust: {{name}} has an arm that concerns me. The velocity has plateaued, the mechanics are effortful, and the breaking ball is a slurvy non-descript pitch. The physical projection has maxed out. I don't see a path forward as a starter or reliever.
Pass on the arm. {{name}} — the fastball is 88-90 and straight, the frame is maxed, and the delivery puts stress on the elbow. Even the raw tools don't project. There's nothing to dream on here.

elite: {{name}} — the numbers are elite across the board. ISO .280+, wRC+ 155, strikeout rate below 18%, walk rate above 12%. This is a complete hitter by every metric. Expected batting average tracks actual within .010. The Statcast data confirms what the traditional stats show: plus hit, plus power, elite plate discipline. Projected WAR: 4.0-6.0 at peak.

Data-driven assessment of {{name}}: 90th percentile exit velocity, 85th percentile sprint speed, chase rate in the bottom 15%. The advanced metrics are uniformly excellent. This isn't a 'tools player who doesn't produce' — this is production backed by elite underlying metrics. The prospect models give him a 75% chance of becoming a 3+ WAR player.

{{name}}'s Statcast profile is absurd. Average exit velocity: 92.3 mph. Barrel rate: 18%. Hard-hit rate: 52%. And the plate discipline metrics are just as impressive — zone contact 88%, chase rate 22%. The numbers say this is an elite hitter. I trust the numbers.
good: {{name}} — solid statistical profile. wRC+ 120-130 range, above-average contact rates, power metrics trending up. The K rate is manageable at 22% and the walk rate is healthy at 9%. Projected outcome: 2.0-3.5 WAR regular. Good value in the first few rounds.

The numbers tell a positive story for {{name}}. Consistent production across multiple levels, with metrics that project well to the majors. Exit velocity is above average (89.5 mph), and the hard-hit rate suggests the power will play. Statistical comparable: {{comp}}.
average: {{name}} — the metrics are mixed. Contact rates are average (75% zone contact) but the quality of contact is below average (85.5 mph exit velo). The power metrics don't project to impact at the major league level. Statistical model gives him a 25% chance of becoming a 1+ WAR player.

Data review of {{name}}: the advanced stats tell a concerning story. High chase rate (33%), below-average walk rate (5%), and a strikeout rate that's rising (28%). The underlying metrics suggest regression from the surface stats. Proceed with caution.
bust: The numbers don't lie, and they're not kind to {{name}}. Contact quality is poor (83.2 mph avg EV), the strikeout rate is alarming (32%), and the expected stats (.210 xBA) are significantly below the actual stats (.255). He's been lucky, not good. Statistical model: 8% chance of meaningful MLB contribution.

{{name}} fails every statistical screen we run. Chase rate: 38% (bottom 5th percentile). Whiff rate: 35%. Expected slugging: .340. The prospect models rank him outside the top 200. I understand other scouts see 'tools' — the data sees a future minor leaguer. Hard pass.

elite: {{name}} — the pitching metrics are outstanding. Stuff+ 130, Location+ 115, Pitching+ 125. Swinging strike rate: 15%. Chase rate induced: 35%. The numbers grade him as an elite arm by every measure. Expected ERA (xERA): 2.85. Projected WAR: 5.0+ at peak. The data is unambiguous.

Statistical deep dive on {{name}}: fastball spin rate 2400 RPM with 18 inches of induced vertical break. That's elite ride. The slider has 3200 RPM with gyro component creating late horizontal sweep. Hitters are batting .150 against the slider. The pitch mix is optimized. This is a data-driven ace.

{{name}}'s metrics are the best I've evaluated this year. K-BB%: 28%. FIP: 2.15. Hard-hit rate allowed: 24% (elite). The Stuff models project three above-average pitches with plus command. Statistical ceiling: Cy Young contender.
good: {{name}} — the numbers support a mid-rotation profile. FIP in the 3.50-3.80 range, K/9 around 8.5, BB/9 under 3.0. The underlying metrics are stable and project well. Not an ace by the numbers, but a reliable 2.5-3.5 WAR contributor.

Solid statistical profile for {{name}}. The pitch metrics grade out as average to above-average across the board. Command metrics are his differentiator — Location+ 110. He suppresses hard contact (28% hard-hit rate) which projects to consistent run prevention.
average: {{name}} — the data is concerning. Stuff+ 95 (below average), high hard-hit rate (38%), declining spin rates. The surface numbers look acceptable but the underlying metrics suggest he's pitching above his talent level. Regression likely.

Statistical review of {{name}}: the fastball metrics are average at best (Stuff+ 100) and the secondary pitches grade below average. The K-BB% of 10% is replacement level. I don't see the data supporting a major league starter profile.
bust: The numbers are brutal for {{name}}. Stuff+ 85, Location+ 88. Expected ERA: 5.50+. Hitters barrel him at a 12% rate (well above average). The spin rates are declining, the velocity is declining, and the command metrics have always been poor. Every statistical indicator points to a non-prospect.

{{name}} fails every pitching model we run. The fastball generates minimal swinging strikes (6% whiff rate — replacement level). The breaking ball has below-average sweep and depth. Batted ball data: 42% hard-hit rate. This is a batting practice pitcher by the numbers. Do not invest.
